video_title: Lawfare Daily: Consent in the Age of AI
gist: A Georgetown computer scientist (Alyssa Redmiles) and two Lawfare editors argue that AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII) is fundamentally a consent and likeness problem, not a detection/authenticity problem, walking through concrete platform failures (Meta's tag-a-friend generation feature, Grok's 'donut glaze' filter-bypass exploit), why training-data filtering and input/output filters barely slow down bad actors, and an emerging legal fork between authenticity-based and harm-based regulation of generative AI outputs.

themes.theme: Detection vs. consent: reframing the AI-nudification problem

insights.claim: The central technical/policy question for AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery (NCII) is not "is this media fake" but "is this media of me" — existing report-and-attestation systems solve neither the search problem (discovering you've been depicted) nor prevention of the content being created in the first place.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles, guest, Georgetown University computer science professor
insights.so_what: Reframes the regulatory and product target from detection/authenticity tooling toward likeness-recognition and prevention infrastructure; deepfake-detection startups should note detection alone doesn't solve NCII.
insights.quote: Is this media fake is different than is this media of me?

insights.claim: Mathematical authenticity distinctions are legally and sociologically irrelevant to NCII harm — a generated image can be pixel-by-pixel completely different from any real photo of a person yet still produce identical reputational and psychological harm because it plays the same social role.
insights.speaker: Kate Klonick, senior editor, Lawfare
insights.so_what: Signals that legal liability frameworks built around 'is this authentic/real' (favoring generative-AI First Amendment defenses) may lose out to harm-based, dignitary-privacy frameworks — a key swing factor for AI-model legal risk.
insights.quote: it is pixel by pixel a completely different image than a photo of Renee

insights.claim: There is no agreed mathematical definition of "likeness," unlike membership inference (recovering exact training-data images), which can be formally defined — this gap is why technical fixes for NCII lag technical fixes for other AI privacy harms.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Explains why NCII-specific safety tooling remains immature relative to other AI-privacy research (e.g., data extraction attacks) — a technical white-space for further research or startups.

themes.theme: Platform failures in practice: Meta, Grok, and the donut-glaze exploit

insights.claim: Meta AI shipped a feature that sent users push notifications encouraging them to generate AI content of other people by tagging their Instagram handle, using that person's public Instagram photos as the generation base layer, without the depicted person's consent or knowledge; the feature was reportedly pulled after protest from creator guilds.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta, senior editor, Lawfare
insights.so_what: Illustrates concrete platform-level product risk in 'tag a person to generate' AI features and shows reputational/regulatory tail risk for similar consumer AI features.

insights.claim: On X/Grok, users discovered that requesting "donut glaze" be applied to a person functioned as a filter-bypass that routed around content moderation to generate extremely explicit sexualized imagery of real, non-consenting individuals directly beneath their posts, and some resulting images received millions of views.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta
insights.so_what: A concrete, named jailbreak pattern (euphemism substitution) that safety teams and competitors should test for; also shows viral-scale distribution risk once an exploit is discovered.
insights.quote: you could ask for donut glaze to be all over someone

insights.claim: The Grok 'donut glaze' exploit triggered EU regulators to request specific data from X, making it one of the few AI-NCII incidents to draw formal regulatory action, even though it received little US mainstream media coverage.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta
insights.so_what: EU regulators appear more responsive to concrete abuse-vector evidence than US press coverage — foreign regulatory action can be a leading indicator of platform policy change even absent domestic pressure.

insights.claim: Ashley St. Clair, an influencer, is suing Elon Musk over Grok-generated sexualized imagery of her; as of this recording the case had seen little docket movement and was reportedly stuck on a venue dispute.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta
insights.so_what: An active, named piece of litigation to track as a potential precedent-setting test of platform liability for AI-generated NCII.

insights.claim: In a forthcoming Center for Democracy and Technology (CDT) report, Stability AI (maker of Stable Diffusion, heavily used on Civitai) reportedly filed zero reports to NCMEC during a one-year study period, while OpenAI filed far more; the researchers argue report volume reflects monitoring intensity rather than actual abuse prevalence.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A concrete, comparable transparency metric for benchmarking model providers' trust-and-safety investment — near-zero NCMEC reporting from an open-weight leader is a red flag for governance diligence.

themes.theme: Why model-level technical fixes don't work yet

insights.claim: Stripping certain content categories (e.g., images of children) from training data does not reliably prevent a model from generating that content — in Redmiles's research it only raised generation difficulty from roughly three prompts to about twelve, and even that partial mitigation applies only to closed-weight models since open-weight models can have the concept reintroduced via fine-tuning.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Quantifies how weak training-data filtering is as a safety control — a concrete metric ('3 to 12 prompts') that undercuts marketing claims by model providers about training-data safeguards.
insights.quote: it kind of makes it go from three prompts worth of difficult to 12 prompts worth of difficult

insights.claim: The dominant safeguard foundation model providers actually deploy is input/output filtering (checking prompts and outputs for policy violations) rather than training-data interventions, but these filters are circumventable and are themselves often powered by unreliable AI classifiers.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: 'AI safety' marketing claims about generation prevention often rest on the weakest, most gameable layer of the stack — relevant for diligence on any 'safe AI image generation' vendor claims.

insights.claim: Anti-tampering and anti-fine-tuning research exists to make it harder to strip safety controls out of open-weight models, but none of it is mature enough for deployment and no platform currently uses it.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Identifies a genuine technical white-space — potential opportunity for AI-safety infrastructure research or tooling.

insights.claim: Open-weight model providers (e.g., those distributing via Hugging Face) do essentially no monitoring of downstream use of their models, and dedicated online hobbyist communities function as informal technical 'upskilling centers' that teach even non-technical users to compress, fine-tune, and run these models locally to create sexual content.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: The technical barrier to producing harmful synthetic content has collapsed from 'requires ML expertise' to 'learnable via community tutorials,' expanding the population of potential bad actors — relevant to any risk model for open-weight model proliferation.

insights.claim: Of 28 people interviewed across AI sexual-content-creation communities, 3 admitted to making non-consensual intimate imagery and several others said they had been asked to make it but declined, indicating NCII creation is a real but minority practice within larger, mostly consensual content-creation communities.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Provides a rare quantified base rate for how common NCII requests/creation are within adjacent legitimate communities, useful for calibrating the scale of the problem versus media narratives.

insights.claim: Moderators of AI-content-creation communities found that citing a platform's Terms of Service prohibition on NCII, and the legal risk posed by the U.S. TAKE IT DOWN Act, were the two most effective justifications for enforcing anti-NCII rules — more effective than ethical appeals, which often drew pushback from members framing moderation as censorship of sexual expression.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Suggests platform-hosting ToS clauses plus clear federal criminal liability are the two most practically effective levers for community self-policing — a template other platforms or regulators could replicate.
insights.quote: Excuse me, you're not supposed to kind of yuck my yum. Don't tell me what to do.

insights.claim: Facial recognition technology's accuracy on AI-generated images is essentially unstudied — the underlying pixel distribution of a generated image differs statistically from a photograph, and there has been 'pretty much no research' on how well standard facial recognition algorithms (like TSA's 1:1 matching) perform on synthetic images.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A concrete research/product gap: any startup claiming facial-recognition-based detection of a person in AI-generated content is operating on an unvalidated assumption.

insights.claim: Because recognizing someone's 'likeness' is inherently a human-perception question rather than a pure biometric-matching one, Redmiles's team borrows psychology methodology — the 'familiar facial recognition task' used to diagnose prosopagnosia, plus vignette surveys — to measure whether a depiction counts as harmful, instead of relying on facial-recognition confidence scores.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A reusable methodological approach for any company or regulator trying to operationalize 'likeness' as a legal/product threshold — psychology-based perception testing rather than pure computer-vision confidence.

themes.theme: The legal fork: authenticity rights vs. harm-based regulation

insights.claim: The unsettled legal question of whether generative AI outputs receive First Amendment protection (playing out in cases like Garcia v. Character Technologies) will likely determine NCII regulation's future: an authenticity-based framing (AI outputs as genuinely new, protected expression) would block much regulation/litigation targeting model outputs, while a harm-based dignitary-privacy framing would open a much broader set of legal remedies regardless of technical origin.
insights.speaker: Kate Klonick
insights.so_what: A binary legal fork that materially changes AI model providers' liability exposure for generated-content harms — worth tracking as a leading indicator for regulatory/litigation risk across the sector.

insights.claim: Property/IP-based legal claims are historically more economically enforceable in court than privacy-based claims, which is why organized interest groups mobilize around IP theories first; Klonick draws a direct parallel to the Napster/Kazaa era, when the RIAA and MPAA sued over clear-cut copyright theft rather than dignitary harm, and predicts NCII/likeness disputes will similarly gravitate toward property-based legal theories.
insights.speaker: Kate Klonick
insights.so_what: Predicts which legal theory (property/IP-style 'likeness theft' vs. privacy/dignitary harm) will actually dominate NCII litigation and case-law development going forward — relevant for legal-tech and media-rights investors.

insights.claim: A proposed update to the EU AI Act would reportedly bar deployment of models capable of generating NCII-type content unless 'sufficient safeguards' are implemented, but Redmiles's research found foundation model providers offer little transparency on how those safeguards are evaluated and remain vague about which threat model they're addressing (direct image generation, code generation, instructions, or API misuse).
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A pending EU rule change that could force disclosure/compliance costs on foundation model providers; watch how 'sufficient safeguard' gets defined, since current vagueness favors incumbents with more legal/compliance resources.

insights.claim: There is currently no clear legal framework defining who is authorized to red-team AI systems for NCII-generation vulnerabilities or what liability protection they have — simply claiming 'I was red-teaming' is not a legal defense, creating ambiguity for both in-house safety teams and outside researchers.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta
insights.so_what: A concrete legal/policy gap: absent a safe-harbor framework for authorized AI safety testing, independent researchers face legal exposure that likely suppresses external red-teaming coverage of these harms.

themes.theme: What actually works: deterrence messaging and corporate governance gaps

insights.claim: Deterrence messaging — warnings shown to a user when their prompt appears to request illegal content (e.g., under the TAKE IT DOWN Act) — has shown strong promise in prior research (previously used for CSAM) and in early NCII studies, yet as of this recording zero of seven foundation model providers studied were deploying it for NCII, with only two having it on their roadmap.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: Flags a specific, evidence-backed, currently-unimplemented intervention; any foundation model provider that ships deterrence messaging for NCII would be visibly ahead of all seven peers studied.
insights.quote: none of the seven foundation model providers we looked at and none of the ones we interview interviewed were doing deterrence messaging for NCI. Two had it on the road map, but nobody else um did.

insights.claim: Redmiles argues NCII policy should target the funding/monetization pipeline and 'purpose-built' tools (websites or fine-tuned models specifically built for nudification, identifiable by keywords, functionality, or training signatures) rather than broadly restricting sexual content generation, because overly broad prohibitions tend to push activity to less-controllable corners of the internet like 4chan or offshore sites.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A specific regulatory design principle — narrow, purpose-built-tool targeting over blanket content bans — that policymakers and platform trust-and-safety teams could use to avoid counterproductive enforcement.

insights.claim: Mainstream platforms (e.g., Google, Facebook) acting as single sign-on providers for nudification/NCII websites confer an implicit 'acceptability stamp' on those sites even without intending to, and ad networks and app-store listings for such apps similarly normalize them — a corporate-governance blind spot DiResta says companies have paid 'lip service' to fixing but continue to fail on.
insights.speaker: Renee DiResta
insights.so_what: An auditable, concrete corporate-governance/ESG diligence item: check whether a platform's SSO, ad network, or app store is inadvertently distributing or legitimizing NCII-generation services.
insights.quote: it gives this kind of like acceptability stamp to the platform even if it's not the intent of the single signon providers

insights.claim: Testing whether an AI system will generate NCII/CSAM-adjacent content requires actually running realistic test prompts (e.g., a face-covered generated image checked for identifiability) rather than relying on self-reported compliance forms, because Redmiles found that in practice compliance forms are often filled out without real testing behind them.
insights.speaker: Alyssa Redmiles
insights.so_what: A due-diligence red flag for anyone evaluating a platform's safety claims: request evidence of actual red-team testing, not just a completed compliance questionnaire.
